danger_signals.meaning: Player has taken damage and is vulnerable; visual differs by region (red flash in JP, light blue in international)
Player character defeated; visual form censored in international versions (blood cloud in JP becomes mist/sweat effect)
Boss vulnerability and defeat state; Death boss loses head in JP version (censored to burning effect in international versions)
Immediate threat; changed from crosses in JP to faces in international versions for regional tone

safety_signals.meaning: Enhanced defensive capability; visual redesigned to be more recognizable cross form in international versions
Safe progression point; absent in international versions, indicating regional censorship of reward feedback

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Castle Dracula as contested territory; rescue mission objective (Annet and Maria as faction allies)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Castle as stronghold; coffin as power source
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Death boss as Dracula's servant; werewolf form indicating corruption
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Medieval Transylvania setting; castle interior with undead inhabitants; dark architectural aesthetic

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Medieval Transylvania landscape; people have forgotten horror
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Depraved individuals able to revive Count; castle remains accessible as dungeon

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Castle interior becomes active hostile environment with undead enemies
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Kidnapping of Annet and Maria triggers Richter's quest; Dracula plants ambushes and deploys Death as lieutenant

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Multiple ending variations based on rescue completion
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Ending differs if both hostages rescued, only Maria rescued, or neither rescued; affects narrative outcome

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Blood effects removed/recolored; title screen animation differs (JP: cross draw-in, NA: blood pour effect, EU: simple fade)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Violence indicators softened in non-Japanese versions; religious symbol handling adjusted (cross becomes less literal)

environmental_storytelling.example: Dracula's castle interior serves as both dungeon and character presence; branching paths suggest player agency in rescue mission routing
Setting 'several hundred years' after Simon Belmont's victory establishes Dracula's immortality and cyclic revival pattern
Annet (girlfriend) and Maria (sister) imprisoned separately, creating two-part rescue objective that affects ending outcomes
Sacred whip passed through Belmont bloodline visualizes generational conflict and supernatural family legacy
Warlock throws tombstones as attacks, visualizing cemetery/death theme and undead servant aesthetic

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Flashes red (JP) or light blue (International); indicates vulnerability window
Disintegrates into blood cloud (JP) or mist particles (International); indicates game over state
Blood splatters appear on impact (white in international versions, red in JP)
Loses head with blood spray (JP) or burns up (International); dramatic defeat animation signifies boss vulnerability
Dying pose received censorship in Advance Collection rerelease, softening violence visual
Undead enemies populate castle interior; movement and attack patterns indicate Dracula's control

notes: Castlevania: Dracula X demonstrates strong regional visual communication censorship, particularly around blood effects, death animations, and religious iconography. The game communicates danger through character damage feedback (color-coded flash effects) and boss destruction states. World-state is communicated through branching level design and multiple endings tied to rescue objectives. Faction identity is implicit through the Belmont legacy (weapon inheritance) versus Dracula's undead empire (castle architecture, subordinate monsters). The game's visual language emphasizes Medieval Gothic horror aesthetic with region-specific modifications that downplay graphic violence in Western releases. Environmental storytelling relies heavily on dungeon progression, enemy placement (tombstone-throwing Warlocks), and narrative signposting (kidnapped hostages as rescue targets).
sources_used: tcrf - Castlevania: Dracula X (The Cutting Room Floor) - regional differences, visual censorship details, boss animations, damage feedback
wikipedia - Castlevania: Dracula X - plot summary, world-state documentation, multiple endings system, gameplay mechanics
